questions.text: A user reports an email urging immediate payroll changes with a link to a look-alike domain. What should the SOC do FIRST?

options.text: Forward the email to personal Gmail for storage
Quarantine similar messages, block the domain at the gateway, and reset credentials if the user submitted data
Wait for three more reports before acting
Reply-all warning employees not to click

questions.explanation: Containment (quarantine/block) and credential safety come before broad communication.

questions.text: Your phishing simulation click rate dropped to zero after blocking all external links. What risk remains?

options.text: No risk remains; email is fully secure
Unsupported OS on printers only
ARP cache poisoning on Wi-Fi
Voice phishing (vishing) and attachment-based malware bypass link filtering

questions.explanation: Link blocking does not stop vishing or weaponized attachments—defense must be layered.

questions.text: An executive receives a QR code poster 'for IT Wi-Fi' in the parking garage. Which category does this BEST fit?

options.text: DNS tunneling exfiltration
SAST finding in CI
Quishing (QR phishing) leading to credential harvesting
Patch management failure

questions.explanation: Malicious QR codes are a growing phishing channel outside email filters.

questions.text: Mail headers show SPF pass, DKIM fail, and DMARC none for a message impersonating the CEO. What improvement is MOST effective?

options.text: Remove DKIM to simplify troubleshooting
Allow any sender if subject contains 'urgent'
Disable SPF globally
Enforce DMARC p=reject with aligned DKIM for all executive domains

questions.explanation: DMARC with alignment stops many spoofed From domains even when SPF alone passes via forwarders.

questions.text: Users see internal display names matching a VP but the SMTP address is external. Which user-facing control helps?

options.text: External sender banners and training to verify the actual email address
Increase font size in Outlook
Disable all calendar invites
Block PDF attachments from HR

questions.explanation: Display-name spoofing is common; banners and address verification reduce mistaken trust.

questions.text: A VoIP caller ID shows your company's main number. What technical control reduces this spoofing vector?

options.text: STIR/SHAKEN attestation on carrier trunks where supported
Publish the main number on Instagram only
Remove VLANs from the PBX
Disable MFA for call center staff

questions.explanation: STIR/SHAKEN improves caller ID integrity for voice spoofing on supported carriers.

questions.text: An invoice attachment is a password-protected ZIP containing a .lnk file. What is the SAFEST handling step?

options.text: Sandbox detonation in an isolated environment before any user execution
Rename the .lnk to .txt to neutralize it
Forward to finance to open on their desktop
Ask the sender for the password in email reply

questions.explanation: Password-protected archives bypass scanners; sandbox analysis avoids user execution risk.

questions.text: Macro-enabled Excel arrives from an unknown vendor address. Which policy is BEST?

options.text: Allow macros if the spreadsheet has a logo
Require users to enable editing on all attachments
Disable antivirus to speed opening
Block macros from internet-origin files and use protected view

questions.explanation: Office macros remain a common initial access path; blocking internet macros is baseline.

questions.text: Email filter quarantined a PDF with embedded JavaScript. Why is this noteworthy?

options.text: JavaScript in PDF only affects printers
Quarantine means the file is guaranteed safe
PDFs can launch actions or download second-stage payloads when opened in vulnerable readers
PDFs cannot contain active content

questions.explanation: Malicious PDF features are a documented attachment risk; quarantine is appropriate.

questions.text: Finance receives wire instructions changing bank details via email right before quarter close. What verification is REQUIRED?

options.text: Process if the logo matches
Out-of-band callback to a known number on file, not numbers in the email
Reply to the same thread asking 'Is this real?'
Trust because the subject says 'CONFIDENTIAL'

questions.explanation: BEC wire fraud relies on urgency; verified voice callback stops many losses.

questions.text: An attacker registers a domain one character off from your vendor. Which control reduces payment fraud?

options.text: Single approver if under $50k
Disable accounts payable entirely
Vendor master data with approved domains and dual approval on banking changes
Whitelist all .com domains

questions.explanation: Look-alike domains are classic BEC; vendor controls and dual approval are key.

questions.text: Legal notices a forged CEO email approving acquisition NDA sharing. Besides legal, who must be engaged FIRST?

options.text: Facilities for parking passes
Patch management team for WSUS
Physical security for CCTV only
Security operations to hunt for mailbox rules forwarding and similar threads

questions.explanation: BEC often includes hidden inbox rules; SOC hunts prevent ongoing exfiltration.
